title: The Works of the Flesh
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 09-22-24 -AM- The Works of the Flesh - Glatians 5_13-25 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Works of the Flesh Are Evident
subpoints: The flesh wants to blur the line between sin and righteousness, but Paul says these works are obvious
The list is not exhaustive — Paul adds "and the like" to cover the lawyer in every heart
These sins are internal, not caused by external people or circumstances

outline.title: The Four Categories of the Works of the Flesh
subpoints: Sexual sins: adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lewdness
Worship sins: idolatry (including the idol of self), sorcery (mind-altering substances)
Relational sins: hatred, contentions, jealousies, outbursts of wrath, selfish ambitions, dissensions, heresies, envy, murder

outline.title: Those Who Practice Such Things Will Not Inherit the Kingdom
subpoints: Paul's warning is language of identity: a life characterized by these sins
Not a one-time failure but a life whose film is defined by these sins
Calvin: "What more terrifying thing can be said than that they who walk after the flesh are banished from the kingdom of God?"

outline.title: Two Solutions
subpoints: If you are outside of Christ: come to him now without cleaning yourself up first; he will change you
If you are in Christ but not walking in the Spirit: believe God is at work in you; repent, put faith into practice, ask for help from someone not struggling with the same sin
You are not a helpless sinner — you are a saint who sins, indwelt by the Holy Spirit

summary: In the second sermon of the Fruit of the Spirit series, Pastor Tito Lyro confronts the uncomfortable subject of the works of the flesh in Galatians 5:13-25, arguing that a clear view of the sinful heart is necessary before the fruit can be properly understood. He organizes Paul's list of seventeen works of the flesh into four categories — sexual sins, worship sins, relational sins, and substance sins — and shows that these are internal to us, not caused by others. The works of the flesh are evident and not complicated, and Paul's warning that those who practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God is language of identity, describing a life characterized by sin. The sermon then turns to two possible solutions: for those outside of Christ, an invitation to come to him as they are; and for believers not walking in the Spirit, an urgent call to repent, to believe that God is at work in them, and to seek accountability from brothers and sisters in the church. Pastor Lyro emphatically rejects the idea that a Christian is merely a helpless sinner, insisting instead that every believer is a saint who sins, indwelt by the Spirit and equipped with grace to resist temptation.
transcript:
## Introduction

For a scripture reading this morning, we're reading from Galatians chapter 5, starting at verse 13, reading through verse 25. This is the second message in our series on the fruit of the Spirit.

*[Scripture reading: Galatians 5:13-25]*

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we thank you for your word. We pray that your Spirit would be present with us, doing a mighty work in our hearts. Father, we pray to be with the words of my mouth that they might be glorifying to you and good to your people. We pray in Jesus' name, Amen. Please be seated.

## Review of the Previous Sermon

Last Lord's Day we saw that the rich soil in which the fruit of the Spirit grows is a life lived in the Spirit — which means a life lived according to what the Spirit says in his Word. In other words, the fruit of the Spirit grows in the obedient heart.

This life of obedience is not necessarily easy, because even though we have been born again and have the Spirit of God dwelling in us, there remains in us a part that is still in rebellion against God — Paul calls that part the flesh. In chapter 5 verse 18 he says the Spirit of God who is in us is fighting against the flesh and the flesh is fighting against the Spirit of God.

It is important to realize that in Paul's account of the flesh battling the Spirit, the believer is not characterized by the flesh. It is not sin that characterizes us, but obedience to the Lord Jesus Christ. And as we make our way toward the more pleasant topic of the actual fruit of the Spirit in verse 22, we must deal with verses 19-21 — the unpleasant subject of the works of the flesh. We need to know where our heart is in relation to Christ and his Spirit.

## The Works of the Flesh Are Evident

In verse 19 we read: *Now the works of the flesh are evident.* The flesh tends to want to blur things. One of its favorite expressions is "it's complicated." And yet there is no mystery to what is fleshly according to Paul. The works of the flesh are evident — obvious, easy to be seen. This means that when we see these things in our lives, we don't have to spend a lot of time trying to figure out if we should repent of them. It is clear.

Now, knowing that we all have little lawyers in our hearts ready to find loopholes, the Holy Spirit gives a list — but then adds the magical words at the end: *and the like.* If the Holy Spirit had given only a list and not added those words, we might say, "Oh, I don't struggle with these things, so I'm fine." The "and the like" tells us this is not an exhaustive list. If you ever wanted an exhaustive list of the works of the flesh, you would need to compile everything not consistent with loving God and loving others, across all 66 books of the Bible, in light of the 10 commandments.

Also notice: *these are the works of the flesh.* They are not external to us. These are not things people are doing to us. They are in us. If we don't own our sins as entirely our fault, we will not be able to repent with a repentance that does not need to be repented of.

## Four Categories of the Works of the Flesh

There are 17 works of the flesh listed here (the NKJV adds adultery and murder to the 15 in the ESV). They fall into four categories.

**Works of the flesh related to sexual activity:** Adultery — sexual relations between two people where one is married. Fornication — better translated "sexual immorality," covering all sexual activity outside of marriage. Uncleanness — unnatural sexual activity. Lewdness — the public flaunting and approval of sexual sins. As Romans 1:32 says, not only is it sinful to do these things, but also to approve of those who do them.

**Works of the flesh related to worship:** Idolatry — the worship of any false god created in the heart. Every time we make a decision based on glorifying self, we are practicing idolatry, because the ultimate idol we all have is self. Sorcery — the word from which we get the English word *pharmaceutical*, because witchcraft was practiced by consuming mind-altering drugs in hopes of accessing the spiritual world. The Bible calls that a work of the flesh.

**Works of the flesh related to relationships** — the bulk of the list, because the Galatians were struggling to get along with one another: hatred, contentions, jealousies (wanting relationships others have to the point of sinning to get them), outbursts of wrath, selfish ambitions (promoting self at the expense of others), dissensions (an oppositional attitude), heresies (causing unnecessary division and schisms), envy (wanting what others have), and murder.

**Works of the flesh related to substances:** Drunkenness — consuming mind-altering substances recreationally, including alcohol and other drugs. Revelries — wild parties involving excess, whether food, alcohol, or other substances. A life controlled by substances is another form of idolatry.

## Those Who Practice Such Things Will Not Inherit the Kingdom

Paul says in verse 21 that those who practice these sins *will not inherit the kingdom of God.* Plain and simple — they will not go to heaven. This is language of identity: if the film of your life is defined by these sins, you are in danger of not inheriting the kingdom of God. John Calvin says, "What more terrifying thing can be said than that they who walk after the flesh are banished from the kingdom of God?"

Those who belong to the kingdom of God have been freed to serve one another through love, and the works of the flesh are not expressions of love for those around us.

## Two Solutions

If you are struggling with any of these works of the flesh dominating your life, there are only two possible explanations.

**First, you may not belong to the kingdom of God.** If your life is characterized by any of these things, you must honestly ask yourself: Am I in Christ? The solution is to come to him — right now. You don't have to reform yourself first. In Isaiah 1 God says: *Come now and let us reason together. Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow.* Believe that Jesus Christ lived the perfect life God demands and that you cannot live, that he died the death you deserved, that his sacrifice was enough to pay the entirety of your debt with God. Repent and trust in Christ.

**Second, you may be in Christ but not continually walking in the Spirit.** Calvin says: "The saints themselves are sometimes heavily burdened, but they return to the way." Believe that God is working in you — Philippians 1:6 says he who began a good work in you will complete it. Believe that you can, by God's grace, obey and resist the flesh. First Corinthians 10:13 says: *No temptation has overtaken you except such as is common to man, and God is faithful, who will not allow you to be tempted beyond what you are able, but with the temptation will also make the way of escape.* Repent. Put what you believe into practice. And ask for help from a brother or sister who is not struggling with the same sin as you.

Brothers and sisters, do not think of yourself as sinners who cannot help but sin. You are saints who sin. You are never identified by your sin. You may struggle with various temptations, but that is not who you are. You are washed by the blood of Christ.

As we walk in the Spirit, our hearts become fertile ground for the fruit of the Spirit. Instead of the works of the flesh, it will be love, joy, peace, patience, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control that characterize our lives — for the glory of Christ and the good of the church.

Glorious God, we thank you for your word. We pray that your word would dwell deeply in our hearts, that we might glorify you in our reliance on you and your grace. We pray in Jesus' name, Amen.
